annotations.text: On the basis of this patient’s history and findings on physical examination, I cannot distinguish between syphilis and Crohn’s disease.
Both diseases cause proctitis, fissures, and perianal papules.
On the basis of the patient’s sexual history, syphilis is more likely than inflammatory bowel disease.
the presence of other anorectal abnormalities in this patient raises doubts about whether he really had hemorrhoids.
If we rule out all these entities from our differential diagnosis, we are left with Crohn’s disease and a number of infectious diseases.
the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
The onset of secondary syphilis typically occurs 4 to 12 weeks after exposure; although symptoms typically resolve in 3 to 12 weeks, they may last for months.
Between January 2013 and January 2014, the number of reported cases of syphilis increased by 15%, and in 2014, a total of 83% of men with primary and secondary syphilis were in the MSM population.
Overall, the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
Does this patient have an underlying infectious disease?
However, rash may go unnoticed by either a patient or a physician.
Rash is a nearly universal feature of secondary syphilis, affecting 93% of MSM with secondary syphilis, so its absence should give us pause.

In this case, although  *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* and  *Chlamydia trachomatis* (serovars D through K) are the two most common causes of proctitis among men who have sex with men (MSM) (accounting for 30% and 19% of all infections, respectively), the incubation periods are too short to fit with the time course of this patient’s possible exposure relative to the onset of symptoms ; the incubation period for gonorrhea is 5 to 10 days, and that for chlamydia is 7 to 10 day
In a review of cases in which MSM presented to a surgery clinic for treatment of proctitis, syphilis was a more common cause than Crohn’s disease.
Syphilitic proctitis is a diagnosis that fits this patient’s presentation and timeline.
The symptoms in this patient most likely reflect secondary syphilis.
In the era before penicillin, secondary syphilis was a common cause of proctitis and of a variety of perianal findings, including fissures and condylomata lata, which can mimic external hemorrhoids.
The relationship between syphilitic proctitis and hepatitis is important to remember and is related to the venous drainage pathway from the rectal area into the portal system.
the titer of rapid plasma reagin had declined from 1:256 to 1:4 after treatment.
